Tier 3 framework-generality runs (5,700, excluding OpenPopulation which is dual-counted in the main pipeline) support framework-generality claims in Appendix G.4.*

# Appendix C: R Analysis Pipeline {-}

```{=latex}
\renewcommand{\thetable}{C.\arabic{table}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{C.\arabic{figure}}
\setcounter{table}{0}
\setcounter{figure}{0}
```

All analysis performed in R (version $\geq$ 4.3). Scripts in `R/` directory, executed from the `CIM_Model/` working directory. All scripts source `R/constants.R` for shared targets and scenario classification (single source of truth):

- `constants.R`: policy targets, winter onset (week 9), scenario classification (`POLICY_LEVERS` holds the original 15 levers + 1 context; the 7 Phase 3 robustness scenarios are classified in the robustness family of `03_hypothesis_tests.R`, plus 1 auxiliary --- 22 levers + 1 context + 1 auxiliary across the v6.4 main pipeline)
- `00_setup.R`: package installation and shared constants
- `00_run_all.R`: execute full pipeline in sequence
- `01_load_data.R`: read and clean NetLogo CSV outputs (last-block deduplication; 23 scenarios as of Phase 3 verification round 2)
- `02_descriptive_stats.R`: summary statistics and bimodality tests
- `03_hypothesis_tests.R`: Welch's t-tests with Holm correction (3 families: 24 confirmatory + 66 exploratory + 42 robustness = 132 tests)
- `04_sensitivity_analysis.R`: rank-based PRCC analysis via `sensitivity::pcc()`
- `05_survival_analysis.R`: Kaplan-Meier and Cox proportional hazards
- `06_visualization.R`: all figures (publication-quality, scenario palette)
- `07_thesis_tables.R`: LaTeX/CSV tables
- `08_network_analysis.R`: degree distribution, assortativity, mixing matrices
- `09_policy_outputs.R`: cost-effectiveness frontier, equity analysis
- `10_validation_table.R`: face validity and pattern validity checks
- `11_agent_hazard.R`: discrete-time dropout hazard (real agent-week panel)
- `12_distributional.R`: quantile ribbons, tail risk, dropout distributions
- `13_surrogate.R`: scenario comparison delta table (surrogate regression removed; information leakage identified, see Limitations)
- `14_internal_invariants.R`: 10 automated mechanism invariant checks (11 sub-checks)
- `15_equifinality.R`: Alternative Mechanism Benchmark (contact-presence contagion)
- `16_tier3_blockj_analysis.R`: gamma-bracket, open-population, and sensitivity-provenance analyses
- `17_tier3_blocki_analysis.R`: Berlin second-domain ranking-invariance analysis
- `18_tier3_integrate.R`: results integration into thesis text
- `19_tier3_splice_and_ship.R`: delivery bundle assembly
- `20_sna_graph_analysis.R`: Structural network analysis from edge files (modularity, betweenness, clustering, weak ties; original 16 scenarios)
- `21_r_naught.R` (Phase 2 Item 3): R$_0$ derivation from observed network statistics across scenarios
- `22_did_parallel_trends.R` (Phase 2 Item 9): Difference-in-differences parallel-trends test for time-varying-treatment scenarios
- `23_randomization_inference.R` (Phase 2 Item 7): randomization-inference robustness check on 24 confirmatory comparisons (10,000 permutations)
- `24_dose_response.R`: dose-response composition sweep with Kendall rank correlation, linear/quadratic regression, AIC + LRT comparison
- `25_link_prediction.R`: static link-prediction with 4 predictors (Adamic-Adar, common neighbours, Jaccard, preferential attachment) on 964 successfully-analysed runs
- `26_open_population_analysis.R`: Welch + Cohen's d on 6 outcomes for OpenPopulation vs Baseline with comparative-magnitude vs H1-H4
- `27_centrality_buddy_analysis.R` (verification round 2): 4-way comparison Baseline / BuddyProgram / RandomBuddy / CentralityBuddy with 36 pairwise tests
- `28_open_pop_sna.R` (Phase 3 verification round 1): multi-metric SNA on Baseline vs OpenPopulation (6 graph-theoretic metrics)
- `29_suboptimal_open_test.R` (Phase 3 verification round 2): 2x2 factorial test of Baseline-vs-Suboptimal ranking-preservation under open-population
- `extra_agent_fate.R`: Agent fate heatmap (individual-level outcome visualisation)

# Appendix D: Full Hypothesis Test Results {-}

```{=latex}
\renewcommand{\thetable}{D.\arabic{table}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{D.\arabic{figure}}
\setcounter{table}{0}
\setcounter{figure}{0}
```

The table below reports all 132 pairwise comparisons (22 non-baseline scenarios across 6 outcomes) with Holm-corrected p-values, applied separately within three pre-specified families to control the family-wise error rate without diluting power across unrelated test sets. The confirmatory family corresponds to H1--H4 (4 scenarios x 6 outcomes = 24 tests). The exploratory family covers the 11 remaining original scenarios (High SES Heterogeneity, Women-Only Groups, NoIndoor Minimal, Targeting50/70/90, BuddyProgram, RotatingGroups, Winter50, WomenChildcare, Low Park Density; 11 x 6 = 66 tests). The auxiliary Equifinality benchmark is reported separately (Appendix G.5) and is not part of this family. The Phase 3 robustness family covers the 7 verification-round extensions (Composition2/3/4 for the dose-response sweep, OpenPopulation, SuboptimalOpen, CentralityBuddy, RandomBuddy; 7 x 6 = 42 tests). Holm correction is computed within each family separately rather than pooled across all 132 tests.

```{=latex}
\input{tables/table_full_tests_appendix.tex}
```


## D.1 Statistical-inference methods and disclosures {-}

*Survival-model specification.* The Cox proportional hazards model (Section 4.3) includes `initial_motivation` as a pre-treatment baseline covariate, assigned at agent creation before any scenario effect applies. Because `initial_motivation` is independent of scenario assignment, it is a legitimate baseline control, not a mediator. Robust standard errors are clustered by simulation run to address within-run correlation between agents sharing the same random seed. The proportional-hazards assumption, tested via Schoenfeld residuals, is rejected at $p < 0.001$ for gender, `initial_motivation`, `distance_to_park`, SES, prior exercise, and scenario assignment, and also for `arrival_cohort` ($p = 0.004$), with the global $\chi^2 = 16{,}122$ on 22 degrees of freedom (migrant-only fit, $N = 405{,}000$ agent-run observations). Two clarifications follow. First, this is a violation of constancy (the hazard ratio varies over the 52-week window), not of internal validity: in the Rubin selection-bias decomposition, the bias term $E[Y_{0i} \mid D_i = 1] - E[Y_{0i} \mid D_i = 0]$ is structurally zero in this design because BehaviorSpace draws independent random seeds for each replication, so the potential-outcome distributions $Y_0$ are identically distributed across scenarios in expectation. The reported hazard ratios therefore retain causal interpretation as time-averaged log-hazard effects of scenario assignment over the 52-week observation window, not constant multiplicative shifts. Second, the Schoenfeld $\chi^2$ is highly sensitive to minor departures from proportionality at this sample size; the magnitudes of the violations are not necessarily scientifically consequential. Time-varying coefficient extensions (interval-split hazards, `tt()` terms in `coxph`) remain a priority for follow-up work. Finally, the model reports its covariate hazard ratios without a formal multiple-comparisons correction across the covariate set; given the sample size ($N = 405{,}000$), the principal predictors (initial motivation, prior exercise, gender, and distance to park) are significant by margins that no standard correction (Holm or Bonferroni) would overturn, though any marginal covariate should be read with that caveat in mind.

**Pre-registration and multiple-comparisons structure.** Hypothesis tests are partitioned into three families, with Holm correction applied separately within each (following peer-review feedback during thesis development, extended for the Phase 3 robustness extensions). The **confirmatory family** comprises the four scenarios that directly implement the four pre-specified hypotheses: Weak Peer Influence (H1), Suboptimal Composition (H2), No Indoor Continuity (H3), and Minimal Support (H4). Across six outcomes (retention, motivation, CEFR gain, cross-group tie ratio, female dropout rate, male dropout rate) this yields $4 \times 6 = 24$ confirmatory comparisons. The **exploratory family** comprises the eleven remaining original scenarios (High SES Heterogeneity, Women-Only Groups, NoIndoor Minimal, Targeting50/70/90, BuddyProgram, RotatingGroups, Winter50, WomenChildcare, Low Park Density), yielding $11 \times 6 = 66$ exploratory comparisons. The **robustness family** comprises the seven Phase 3 robustness-extension scenarios (Composition2, Composition3, Composition4, OpenPopulation, SuboptimalOpen, CentralityBuddy, RandomBuddy; the latter two added in the verification round to disentangle timing from criterion in Item 10, and SuboptimalOpen added to test the open-cohort ranking-preservation conjecture), yielding $7 \times 6 = 42$ robustness comparisons. Separating the three families preserves family-wise error rate at $\alpha = 0.05$ within each interpretive category and avoids the dilution of confirmatory power that arises when interpretively distinct tests are pooled under a single Holm correction. The split is implemented in `R/03_hypothesis_tests.R`.

**Causal-inference scaffolding.** The model deliberately violates the agent-level Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption (SUTVA): peer influence is the mechanism of interest, not a nuisance, so an agent's outcome depends on the treatments received by other agents in the same training group. We therefore treat the simulation **run** as the unit of analysis (300 independent random seeds per scenario; standard errors clustered by `run_id` throughout the survival analysis), at which level SUTVA holds by construction since seeds are independent. Reported scenario contrasts are intent-to-treat (ITT) estimates of the effect of *assigning* a cohort to a scenario configuration; under the model's perfect compliance at the run level, run-level ITT and run-level average treatment effect coincide. The agent-level intent-to-treat refinement noted under *Survivor selection* above would require an additional cohort-conditioning specification and is reserved for follow-up work.

# Appendix E: Model Configuration Files {-}

```{=latex}
\renewcommand{\thetable}{E.\arabic{table}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{E.\arabic{figure}}
\setcounter{table}{0}
\setcounter{figure}{0}
```

## Framework Generality {-}

The model is structured as three loosely coupled layers. The **mechanism layer**, implemented in `CIM_v6_4.nlogo`, encodes the domain-agnostic dynamics of structured community-sport programmes: peer-mediated motivation dynamics, threshold-based dropout, tie formation and decay, seasonal participation disruption, and language acquisition through repeated co-presence with native speakers. The **configuration layer** is a CSV file (\path{config/calisthenics-istanbul.csv}) that documents every empirically calibrated or heuristically chosen parameter with its value, unit, admissible range, source, description, calibration tier, and scenario scope. The **case layer** is the application of the mechanism layer under a specific configuration: in this paper, Istanbul calisthenics parks during the 2013--2022 Temporary Protection period.

This layering is the architectural response to a suggestion received during thesis review from the program director: that the simulator, not only the analyses derived from it, is a primary contribution, and should therefore be general enough to admit configurations for other community-sport contexts (e.g., an adult language-café cohort, a community boxing programme, a youth football league). At setup the model loads the selected domain configuration: it first applies an inline copy of the named domain (guaranteeing correct behaviour in every environment, including the sandboxed GUI), then, where file access is available (e.g. headless runs), reads \path{config/<domain>.csv} from disk so that an edited CSV overrides the inline values. The release ships two worked configurations: \path{config/calisthenics-istanbul.csv} (the calibrated Istanbul case) and \path{config/language-course-berlin.csv} (the second-domain transfer test of Appendix G.4), with the file format, column semantics, and an adaptation guide documented in \path{config/schema.md}. Loading \path{config/calisthenics-istanbul.csv} reproduces the hardcoded defaults bit-identically on seeded runs, which preserves the validation results reported in Section 3.10.

Parameters in the configuration file are classified into three **calibration tiers**. *Empirical* parameters (e.g., motivation decay rate $\gamma$, peer-influence coefficient $\beta$) carry a citation to a specific published study from which the value is derived. *Heuristic* parameters (e.g., distance penalty, budget per park) carry an urban-planning or programme-design rationale without a direct empirical source. *Policy-lever* parameters (e.g., group composition, indoor facility probability) are design choices that are manipulated across the scenario set. This classification is intended to make transparent to the reader which values bear the weight of empirical evidence and which are scope choices, and to signal to a researcher adapting the framework to a new domain which parameters are most critical to recalibrate.




The Istanbul-calisthenics calibration is fully specified in \path{config/calisthenics-istanbul.csv} and the Berlin-language-course second-domain calibration in \path{config/language-course-berlin.csv}. Both files share an 8-column schema (name, value, unit, range, source, description, calibration-tier, scenario-scope) and are machine-readable inputs to the model's configuration loader. The two tables below reproduce the full content of both files (44 parameters each) so that a reader can verify every parameter value used in the simulation without consulting the repository. Each parameter is classified into one of three calibration tiers: *empirical* (derived from a cited study), *heuristic* (urban-planning or programme-design rationale without direct empirical source), or *policy-lever* (design choice manipulated across scenarios).

```{r appendix_e_istanbul, echo=FALSE}
istanbul_cfg <- readr::read_csv("config/calisthenics-istanbul.csv", show_col_types = FALSE,
                                col_types = readr::cols(.default = "c"))
istanbul_disp <- istanbul_cfg |>
  dplyr::select(Parameter = name, Value = value, Unit = unit, Range = range,
                Source = source, Tier = `calibration-tier`)
knitr::kable(istanbul_disp, booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, format = "latex",
             align = c("l","r","l","l","l","l"), escape = TRUE,
             caption = "Calisthenics-Istanbul configuration: full inventory of all 44 parameters.", caption.short = "Calisthenics-Istanbul configuration (44 parameters)") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 6, latex_options = c("repeat_header"),
                            repeat_header_continued = "\\textit{(continued)}") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(1, width = "3.0cm") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(2, width = "1.3cm") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(3, width = "1.4cm") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(4, width = "1.5cm") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(5, width = "3.3cm") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(6, width = "1.4cm")
```

```{r appendix_e_berlin, echo=FALSE}
berlin_cfg <- readr::read_csv("config/language-course-berlin.csv", show_col_types = FALSE,
                              col_types = readr::cols(.default = "c"))
berlin_disp <- berlin_cfg |>
  dplyr::select(Parameter = name, Value = value, Unit = unit, Range = range,
                Source = source, Tier = `calibration-tier`)
knitr::kable(berlin_disp, booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, format = "latex",
             align = c("l","r","l","l","l","l"), escape = TRUE,
             caption = "Language-course-Berlin configuration: full inventory of all 44 parameters.", caption.short = "Language-course-Berlin configuration (44 parameters)") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 6, latex_options = c("repeat_header"),
                            repeat_header_continued = "\\textit{(continued)}") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(1, width = "3.0cm") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(2, width = "1.3cm") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(3, width = "1.4cm") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(4, width = "1.5cm") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(5, width = "3.3cm") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(6, width = "1.4cm") |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = "Second-domain calibration used in the framework-generality test reported in Appendix G.4 (Berlin BAMF integration-course case).",
    general_title = "Note: ",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE, threeparttable = TRUE, escape = TRUE
  )
```

The configuration loader in \path{CIM_v6_4.nlogo} (procedure \path{apply-config-value}) accepts these key-value pairs and applies them at setup; a regression test confirms that running with \path{config/calisthenics-istanbul.csv} produces behaviour bit-identical to the hardcoded-default code path on seeded runs, preserving validation results reported in Section 3.10.

# Appendix F: Phase 3 / Robustness Analysis Tables {-}

```{=latex}
\renewcommand{\thetable}{F.\arabic{table}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{F.\arabic{figure}}
\setcounter{table}{0}
\setcounter{figure}{0}
```

This appendix reproduces the contents of every result table referenced inline elsewhere in the thesis: (F.1) randomization-inference robustness, (F.2) dose-response trend tests, (F.3) dose-response per-dose summary, (F.4) 4-way buddy-design comparison, (F.5) link-prediction AUCs, (F.6) link-prediction significance tests, (F.7) open-population multi-metric SNA, (F.8) PRCC values derived from the 810-run Sensitivity_3level sweep, (F.9) link-prediction per-run AUC quartile summary, and (F.10) open-cohort ranking-preservation tests (SuboptimalOpen). The randomization-inference summary statistics are reproduced in F.1; the underlying permutation distributions (10,000 samples per test) are non-tabular and are not reproduced inline.

## F.1. Randomization-inference vs Welch+Holm comparison (24 confirmatory tests) {-}

```{r appendix_f_ri, echo=FALSE}
ri_tbl <- readr::read_csv("tables/table_ri_robustness.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Scenario = scenario,
    Outcome = outcome,
    `Diff` = round(diff_obs, 4),
    `p Welch (Holm)` = formatC(p_welch_holm, format = "g", digits = 3),
    `Sig Welch` = ifelse(sig_welch_holm, "Yes", "No"),
    `p RI (Holm)` = formatC(p_ri_holm, format = "g", digits = 3),
    `Sig RI` = ifelse(sig_ri_holm, "Yes", "No"),
    Agree = ifelse(agree_sig, "Yes", "No")
  )
knitr::kable(ri_tbl, booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, format = "latex",
             align = c("l","l","r","r","l","r","l","l"), escape = TRUE,
             caption = "Randomization-inference vs Welch + Holm comparison.", caption.short = "Randomization-inference vs. Welch+Holm") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 7, latex_options = c("repeat_header"),
                            repeat_header_continued = "\\textit{(continued)}") |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = "All 24 confirmatory comparisons (4 scenarios x 6 outcomes). 10,000 permutations each. Holm correction applied within the confirmatory family. RI agreement with Welch+Holm: 24 of 24.",
    general_title = "Note: ",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE, threeparttable = TRUE, escape = TRUE
  )
```

## F.2. Dose-response: Mann-Kendall and quadratic-vs-linear AIC (4 outcomes) {-}

```{r appendix_f_dose_trend, echo=FALSE}
dt <- readr::read_csv("tables/table_dose_trend_tests.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Outcome = outcome,
    `Kendall tau` = round(kendall_tau, 3),
    `tau p` = formatC(kendall_pvalue, format = "g", digits = 3),
    `LR slope` = round(lr_slope, 4),
    `LR SE` = round(lr_se, 4),
    `R^2` = round(lr_r2, 3),
    `AIC linear` = round(aic_linear, 2),
    `AIC quad` = round(aic_quadratic, 2),
    `Delta AIC` = round(delta_aic, 2),
    `LRT p` = formatC(lrt_pvalue, format = "g", digits = 3)
  )
knitr::kable(dt, booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, format = "latex",
             align = c("l", rep("r", 9)), escape = TRUE,
             caption = "Dose-response trend tests.", caption.short = "Dose-response trend tests") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 7, latex_options = c("repeat_header"),
                            repeat_header_continued = "\\textit{(continued)}") |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = "Locals-per-park dose: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Negative Delta AIC favours linear; positive favours quadratic.",
    general_title = "Note: ",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE, threeparttable = TRUE, escape = TRUE
  )
```

## F.3. Dose-response: per-dose outcome means {-}

```{r appendix_f_dose_resp, echo=FALSE}
dr <- readr::read_csv("tables/table_dose_response.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Dose = dose,
    Scenario = scenario,
    N = n,
    `Retention %` = round(retention_mean, 2),
    `Retention SE` = round(retention_se, 3),
    `Motivation` = round(motivation_mean, 4),
    `CEFR` = round(cefr_mean, 4),
    `Cross-tie` = round(tie_mean, 4)
  )
knitr::kable(dr, booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, format = "latex",
             align = c("r","l","r","r","r","r","r","r"), escape = TRUE,
             caption = "Dose-response per-dose summary statistics.", caption.short = "Dose-response per-dose summary") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 7, latex_options = c("repeat_header"),
                            repeat_header_continued = "\\textit{(continued)}")
```

## F.4. Four-way buddy-design comparison {-}

```{r appendix_f_buddy, echo=FALSE}
cb <- readr::read_csv("tables/table_centrality_buddy.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Comparison = label,
    Outcome = outcome,
    `Mean A` = round(mean_a, 3),
    `Mean B` = round(mean_b, 3),
    `Diff` = round(diff, 3),
    `Diff %` = round(diff_pct, 2),
    `d` = round(cohens_d, 3),
    `p (Holm)` = formatC(p_adj_holm, format = "g", digits = 3),
    Sig = ifelse(sig_holm, "*", "")
  )
knitr::kable(cb, booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, format = "latex",
             align = c("l","l","r","r","r","r","r","r","l"), escape = TRUE,
             caption = "4-way buddy-design comparison: 36 pairwise tests.", caption.short = "Four-way buddy-design comparison (36 tests)") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 6, latex_options = c("repeat_header"),
                            repeat_header_continued = "\\textit{(continued)}") |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = "Six pairwise contrasts (Baseline vs each of {BuddyProgram, RandomBuddy, CentralityBuddy}, plus {BuddyProgram, RandomBuddy, CentralityBuddy} pairwise) x 6 outcomes = 36 tests. Holm correction within the Item-10 family.",
    general_title = "Note: ",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE, threeparttable = TRUE, escape = TRUE
  )
```

## F.5. Link-prediction per-scenario AUCs {-}

```{r appendix_f_lp, echo=FALSE}
lp <- readr::read_csv("tables/table_link_prediction.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Scenario = scenario,
    `N runs` = n_runs,
    `AA mean` = round(auc_aa_mean, 3),
    `AA SE` = round(auc_aa_se, 4),
    `CN mean` = round(auc_cn_mean, 3),
    `CN SE` = round(auc_cn_se, 4),
    `JC mean` = round(auc_jc_mean, 3),
    `JC SE` = round(auc_jc_se, 4),
    `PA mean` = round(auc_pa_mean, 3),
    `PA SE` = round(auc_pa_se, 4)
  )
knitr::kable(lp, booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, format = "latex",
             align = c("l", rep("r", 9)), escape = TRUE,
             caption = "Link-prediction AUCs per scenario.", caption.short = "Link-prediction AUCs per scenario") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 7, latex_options = c("repeat_header"),
                            repeat_header_continued = "\\textit{(continued)}") |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = "AA = Adamic-Adar; CN = common neighbours; JC = Jaccard; PA = preferential attachment. Suboptimal Composition n=69 reflects the 25-edge analysis-stability filter.",
    general_title = "Predictors: ",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE, threeparttable = TRUE, escape = TRUE
  )
```

## F.6. Link-prediction significance tests vs chance (AUC = 0.5) {-}

```{r appendix_f_lp_tests, echo=FALSE}
lpt <- readr::read_csv("tables/table_link_prediction_tests.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Scenario = scenario,
    Predictor = predictor,
    N = n,
    `Mean AUC` = round(mean_auc, 3),
    `t` = round(t_stat, 2),
    `p` = formatC(p_value, format = "g", digits = 3)
  )
knitr::kable(lpt, booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, format = "latex",
             align = c("l","l","r","r","r","r"), escape = TRUE,
             caption = "Link-prediction one-sample t-tests against null AUC = 0.5.", caption.short = "Link-prediction t-tests vs. AUC 0.5") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 7, latex_options = c("repeat_header"),
                            repeat_header_continued = "\\textit{(continued)}")
```

## F.7. Open-population multi-metric SNA {-}

```{r appendix_f_opensna, echo=FALSE}
ops <- readr::read_csv("tables/table_open_pop_sna.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Metric = metric,
    `N base` = n_baseline,
    `N open` = n_openpop,
    `Baseline` = round(mean_baseline, 4),
    `OpenPop` = round(mean_openpop, 4),
    `Diff` = round(diff, 4),
    `d` = round(cohens_d, 3),
    Magnitude = d_magnitude,
    `p (Holm)` = formatC(p_adj_holm, format = "g", digits = 3),
    Sig = ifelse(sig_holm, "*", "")
  )
knitr::kable(ops, booktabs = TRUE, format = "latex",
             align = c("l", rep("r", 6), "l", "r", "l"), escape = TRUE,
             caption = "Multi-metric SNA: closed-cohort Baseline vs open-cohort OpenPopulation.", caption.short = "Multi-metric SNA: closed vs. open cohort") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 7, latex_options = "hold_position") |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = "All graph-structure metrics show negligible effect sizes; none significant after Holm correction within the 6-test family.",
    general_title = "Note: ",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE, threeparttable = TRUE, escape = TRUE
  )
```

## F.8. Sensitivity_3level PRCC summary (CIM v6.4 provenance) {-}

```{r appendix_f_sensitivity, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
library(sensitivity)
sens <- read.csv("data/Sensitivity/sensitivity_table.csv", skip = 6, header = TRUE, check.names = TRUE)
params <- c("motivation.decay.rate", "peer.influence.coefficient",
            "tie.formation.probability", "dropout.threshold")
outcomes <- c("retention.rate.percent", "avg.motivation.level",
              "avg.language.proficiency", "cross.group.tie.ratio", "total.dropouts")
X <- sens[, params, drop = FALSE]
prcc_rows <- lapply(outcomes, function(o) {
  y <- sens[[o]]
  res <- sensitivity::pcc(X, y, rank = TRUE, nboot = 0)
  prcc_vals <- res$PRCC[, "original"]
  data.frame(Outcome = o,
             gamma = round(prcc_vals[1], 3),
             beta  = round(prcc_vals[2], 3),
             p_tie = round(prcc_vals[3], 3),
             theta = round(prcc_vals[4], 3))
})
prcc_tbl <- do.call(rbind, prcc_rows)
prcc_tbl$Outcome <- c("Retention rate (\\%)", "Mean motivation",
                      "Mean CEFR gain", "Cross-group tie ratio", "Total dropouts")
colnames(prcc_tbl) <- c("Outcome", "$\\gamma$ (decay)", "$\\beta$ (peer)",
                        "$p_{\\mathrm{tie}}$", "$\\theta_d$ (dropout)")
knitr::kable(prcc_tbl, booktabs = TRUE, format = "latex", escape = FALSE,
             align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r"),
             caption = "PRCC values per outcome and parameter.", caption.short = "PRCC values per outcome and parameter") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 8, latex_options = "hold_position") |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = "Partial rank correlation coefficients computed via sensitivity::pcc() with rank=TRUE on the 810-run Sensitivity_3level sweep ($3^4 = 81$ parameter combinations x 10 replicates each), run under CIM_v6_4.nlogo; Appendix G.4 documents that the estimate is version-invariant across v6.2--v6.4.",
    general_title = "Source: ",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE, threeparttable = TRUE, escape = TRUE
  )
```

## F.9. Link-prediction per-run AUC distribution summary {-}

```{r appendix_f_lp_runs, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
lp_runs <- readr::read_csv("tables/table_link_prediction_runs.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
lp_long <- lp_runs |>
  dplyr::select(scenario, run, auc_aa, auc_cn, auc_jc, auc_pa) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("auc_"),
                      names_to = "predictor", values_to = "auc") |>
  dplyr::mutate(predictor = dplyr::recode(predictor,
                                          auc_aa = "Adamic-Adar",
                                          auc_cn = "Common neighbours",
                                          auc_jc = "Jaccard",
                                          auc_pa = "Pref. attachment"))
lp_summary <- lp_long |>
  dplyr::group_by(Scenario = scenario, Predictor = predictor) |>
  dplyr::summarise(N = dplyr::n(),
                   Min = round(min(auc, na.rm = TRUE), 3),
                   Q1 = round(quantile(auc, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE), 3),
                   Median = round(median(auc, na.rm = TRUE), 3),
                   Q3 = round(quantile(auc, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE), 3),
                   Max = round(max(auc, na.rm = TRUE), 3),
                   .groups = "drop")
knitr::kable(lp_summary, booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, format = "latex",
             align = c("l", "l", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r"), escape = TRUE,
             caption = "Per-run AUC distribution: 5-number summary per scenario x predictor.", caption.short = "Per-run AUC distribution summary") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 7, latex_options = c("repeat_header"),
                            repeat_header_continued = "\\textit{(continued)}") |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = "Derived from the per-run link-prediction analysis (964 successfully-analysed runs across 4 scenarios; Suboptimal Composition has N=69 after the 25-edge analysis-stability filter). Triadic-closure predictors (Adamic-Adar, common neighbours, Jaccard) all cluster around AUC 0.75 across scenarios; preferential attachment is at chance.",
    general_title = "Note: ",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE, threeparttable = TRUE, escape = TRUE
  )
```

## F.10. Open-cohort ranking-preservation tests (SuboptimalOpen, Phase 3) {-}

```{r appendix_f_subopt_open, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
so_tbl <- readr::read_csv("tables/table_suboptimal_open.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  dplyr::mutate(outcome = dplyr::recode(outcome,
                  retention_rate       = "Retention (%)",
                  avg_motivation       = "Motivation",
                  avg_language_cefr    = "CEFR gain",
                  cross_group_tie_ratio = "Cross-tie ratio",
                  female_dropout_rate  = "Female dropout (%)",
                  male_dropout_rate    = "Male dropout (%)")) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Comparison = paste(scenario_a, "vs", scenario_b),
    Outcome = outcome,
    `Mean A` = round(mean_a, 3),
    `Mean B` = round(mean_b, 3),
    `Diff` = round(diff, 3),
    `d` = round(cohens_d, 3),
    `p (Holm)` = formatC(p_adj_holm, format = "g", digits = 3),
    Sig = ifelse(sig_holm, "*", "")
  )
knitr::kable(so_tbl, booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, format = "latex",
             align = c("l","l","r","r","r","r","r","l"), escape = TRUE,
             caption = "Open-cohort ranking-preservation tests: SuboptimalOpen and OpenPopulation contrasts across six outcomes.", caption.short = "Open-cohort ranking-preservation tests") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 6, latex_options = c("repeat_header"),
                            repeat_header_continued = "\\textit{(continued)}") |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = "Six pairwise contrasts (Suboptimal, OpenPopulation, and SuboptimalOpen vs Baseline, plus the three open-vs-closed and suboptimal-vs-open pairs) x 6 outcomes = 36 tests. Holm correction within the 4-scenario open-cohort family. The Baseline > Suboptimal retention ranking is preserved in both closed and open regimes (Appendix G.4).",
    general_title = "Note: ",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE, threeparttable = TRUE, escape = TRUE
  )
```


# Appendix G: Extended and Robustness Analyses {-}

```{=latex}
\renewcommand{\thetable}{G.\arabic{table}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{G.\arabic{figure}}
\setcounter{table}{0}
\setcounter{figure}{0}
```

This appendix gathers the extended and robustness analyses that support the main findings without forming part of the core argument; each section is a consistency check on a claim made in the body, not a new result. Sections G.1--G.3 document the scenario and network evidence behind Section 4: the eleven exploratory policy designs and the buddy-programme premium (G.1), the link-prediction test showing that ties form through triadic closure rather than degree (G.2), and the structural analysis confirming that the bridging cross-group ties are weak ties in Granovetter's sense (G.3). Sections G.4 and G.5 bound how far the results travel, reporting the gamma-bracket, open-population, and Berlin second-domain checks on framework-generality (G.4) and the alternative-mechanism benchmark behind the equifinality finding (G.5). Section G.6 reports the difference-in-differences and randomization-inference checks that corroborate the confirmatory hypothesis tests.

## G.1 Extended Policy Scenarios {-}

Beyond the four primary hypotheses, eleven additional scenarios explore targeting precision, social facilitation designs, infrastructure modifications, and gender-equity combinations (Table 4.3).

**Targeting accuracy (Targeting50/70/90).** Improving programme targeting from 50% to 90% accuracy in identifying low-SES migrants progressively recovers retention: 40.8%, 41.4%, and 42.6% respectively, though even Targeting90 remains significantly below Baseline (45.1%; $d = -0.43$, $p < 0.001$). The dose-response pattern is consistent with imperfect administrative targeting partially undermining support-service effectiveness, and suggests that 90% accuracy recovers much of the retention benefit at lower administrative overhead than universal provision, without fully closing the gap to Baseline. The scenario set varies targeting accuracy rather than targeted-versus-uniform provision; a controlled targeted-vs-uniform comparison would require an additional scenario.

**Buddy programme.** BuddyProgram (48.5% retention) is the highest-performing of the eleven exploratory-family scenarios, exceeding Baseline by 3.4 percentage points and achieving a 57.7% joint target probability (vs. 49.7% for Baseline). The 8-week structured buddy pairing between a local and a migrant provides a consistent motivation anchor without requiring additional park infrastructure; the cost per retained participant (\euro{}2,929) is comparable to Baseline (\euro{}3,155), making it the highest value-for-money design modification tested in the exploratory family. The Phase 3 robustness extension *CentralityBuddy* (Item 10, immediately below) raises retention further to 50.6% by retargeting the buddy at week 8 to the highest-friendship-degree local in each training group.

**Targeted buddy pairing (CentralityBuddy + RandomBuddy control).** A natural extension of the BuddyProgram intervention asks whether the *selection rule* for buddies matters: the existing setup-time, distance-based pairing (each migrant is matched with the geographically nearest local in the same training group at week 0) versus a degree-based pairing executed once initial social structure has formed. The CentralityBuddy scenario implements the latter: at week 8, after eight weeks of co-attendance have produced a non-trivial degree distribution among the local pool, each non-dropped migrant is paired with the highest-friendship-degree local in their training group. Both buddy variants apply an 8-week $+15$% attendance boost; they differ in pairing TIME (week 0 vs week 8) and CRITERION (nearest distance vs highest friendship degree). To isolate these two dimensions, we add a third arm, **RandomBuddy**: random local selection at week 8 (same timing as CentralityBuddy, same dose, no degree information used). The 4-way comparison disambiguates timing from criterion. Across 300 runs each, retention is 45.1% (Baseline), 48.5% (BuddyProgram, week-0 distance), 50.6% (RandomBuddy, week-8 random), and 50.6% (CentralityBuddy, week-8 degree); cost per retained participant is \euro{}3,155 (Baseline), \euro{}2,929 (BuddyProgram), \euro{}2,806 (RandomBuddy), and \euro{}2,812 (CentralityBuddy). Within the 36-test Item-10 family (6 pairwise contrasts $\times$ 6 outcomes), all three buddy variants beat Baseline on retention (Holm-adjusted $p < 10^{-4}$), and both week-8 variants beat the week-0 BuddyProgram on retention by approximately the same amount (RandomBuddy vs BuddyProgram: $+2.2$ pp, $d = 0.37$, $p < 10^{-3}$; CentralityBuddy vs BuddyProgram: $+2.1$ pp, $d = 0.36$, $p < 10^{-3}$). Critically, **CentralityBuddy and RandomBuddy do not differ significantly on any of the six outcomes** (retention contrast $-0.07$ pp, Cohen's $d = -0.012$, Holm-adjusted $p = 1.00$). The +2.1 pp gap between week-8 buddies and week-0 BuddyProgram is therefore driven by pairing TIME, not pairing CRITERION: a randomly chosen mid-programme buddy is statistically indistinguishable from a degree-targeted mid-programme buddy on every outcome we measure. The mechanism is consistent with self-selection: by week 8 the marginal-dropout candidates have already left or stabilised, so pairing the survivors locks in their attendance through the week-15 cascade window regardless of which specific local they are matched with. We caveat that "self-selection of survivors" is *one* mechanism consistent with this observation, not the only one. Two alternative mechanisms are not separately identified by this design: (i) the week-8 boost lands on the indoor-season-onset cascade window (week 9 onset, weeks 9--16 dropout cluster), so *timing-relative-to-cascade* could be the active driver rather than survivor-pool composition; (ii) at week 8 the friendship graph has accumulated eight weeks of tie-strength growth (cfg-tie-strength-growth = 0.03/week), so *graph-density-at-pairing* could be the active driver. Isolating these would require a third control arm (e.g., week-12 random buddy, or a parameter sweep over the pairing week). This finding does *not* reproduce the high-degree-targeting effect documented by @kim2015 in a Honduran cluster RCT (where friendship-paradox-sampled targets outperformed random targets on health-behaviour diffusion); we attribute the divergence to two design differences: (i) the model's small training groups and low tie-formation rate keep cross-group degree low and compress its heterogeneity, suppressing the degree variation that gives high-degree targeting its leverage in @kim2015, and (ii) the active mechanism here is attendance retention rather than information diffusion, for which timing matters more than network position. The full 4-way comparison (36 pairwise tests) is reported in Appendix F.4; the figure below shows all six outcomes side-by-side.

\FloatBarrier

```{r fig_centrality_buddy, echo=FALSE, fig.scap="Four-way buddy-design comparison", fig.cap="Four-way buddy-design comparison. BuddyProgram (week-0 distance-based pairing), RandomBuddy (week-8 random selection), CentralityBuddy (week-8 degree-based pairing), and Baseline (no buddy). All three buddy variants apply an identical 8-week $+15\\%$ attendance boost; they differ only in TIME (week 0 vs week 8) and CRITERION (distance vs random vs degree). Bars: mean across 300 runs each; error bars: 95\\% CI. Both week-8 variants outperform the week-0 BuddyProgram by about $+2.1$ pp retention, while CentralityBuddy and RandomBuddy are statistically indistinguishable on all six outcomes: the active ingredient is pairing TIME, not pairing CRITERION. Full tests are reported in Appendix F.4.", fig.pos="H", out.width="92%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig_centrality_buddy.png")
```

**Rotating groups.** RotatingGroups (41.7% retention) slightly underperforms Baseline but achieves the highest cross-group tie ratio (0.528 vs. 0.502), trading retention depth for network breadth.

**Winter coverage (Winter50).** 50% indoor partner coverage recovers most winter retention loss (42.3% vs. 34.2% for full No Indoor), confirming a non-linear dose-response for indoor coverage.

**Women-Only with Childcare (WomenChildcare).** This combined intervention achieves 45.8% retention (comparable to Baseline) and brings the gender dropout gap to near-parity: female dropout (54.6%) and male dropout (54.0%) differ by only 0.5 percentage points -- the smallest gender gap of any scenario, and within Monte-Carlo noise, so it is best read as parity rather than a directional advantage. Within the model's scope (women already attending calisthenics-park programmes), universal childcare fully compensates for the reduced cross-gender peer reinforcement in women-only spaces. This result speaks to programme-design choices conditional on attendance; it does not address the prior, culturally and venue-conditioned decision of whether to attend at all.

**NoIndoor Minimal.** Stacking No Indoor Continuity with Minimal Support produces the worst outcomes of all scenarios (28.4% retention, 0% joint target probability), confirming strong negative interaction effects: the two barriers compound rather than simply add.

Agent-level dropout timing clusters sharply at the week-9 winter onset across all scenarios (Figure below, dashed line at week 9). Dropout clustering at weeks 9--16 is visible in all outdoor scenarios, confirming the 2-week mechanism lag between season transition and threshold exceedance.

\FloatBarrier

```{r fig_dropout_dist, echo=FALSE, fig.scap="Dropout timing distribution by scenario", fig.cap="Dropout timing distribution by scenario (ridgeline density plot). Each ridge shows when agents drop out during the 52-week programme. Colour gradient: purple = early dropout, yellow = late dropout. Rows sorted by median dropout week (latest at top). Labels show scenario name and cumulative dropout rate. Dashed lines mark winter onset (week 9) and winter end (week 28). Scenarios with sharp purple peaks exhibit winter-onset cascades; broad yellow ridges indicate gradual attrition throughout the year.", out.width="100%", fig.pos="H"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/12_dropout_distribution.png")
```

Mapping every migrant agent's fate exposes a shifting "survival landscape": the region of initial conditions (motivation and SES) that leads to programme completion contracts sharply under the worst designs.

\FloatBarrier

```{r fig_agent_fate, echo=FALSE, fig.scap="Agent fate map", fig.cap="Agent fate map. Each dot represents one migrant agent (75 per panel). Horizontal position = initial motivation; vertical position = socioeconomic status (SES). Colour encodes dropout week (dark purple = early dropout; bright yellow = late dropout). Black-outlined yellow dots = survived to week 52. Panels sorted by retention rate (worst top-left, best bottom-right). One representative run per scenario (closest to median retention).", out.width="100%", fig.pos="H"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/agent_fate_map.png")
```

Plotted as a cost-retention trade-off against Baseline, the eight additional scenarios split cleanly: BuddyProgram (+3.4 pp retention, -EUR 226 cost) and WomenChildcare (+0.7 pp, -EUR 49) both improve retention and reduce cost, while NoIndoor Minimal and Suboptimal Composition are firmly dominated.

\FloatBarrier

```{r fig_new_tradeoff, echo=FALSE, fig.scap="Additional scenarios: retention vs. cost change", fig.cap="Additional scenarios: retention change (pp) and cost change (EUR) relative to Baseline. Upper-left quadrant = better retention and lower cost than Baseline. BuddyProgram (+3.4 pp, -EUR 226) and WomenChildcare (+0.7 pp, -EUR 49) are the two scenarios in the dominant upper-left quadrant; BuddyProgram has the largest retention uplift. Dashed lines at origin = Baseline.", out.width="90%", fig.pos="H"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig_new_scenarios_tradeoff.png")
```


## G.2 Network Analysis {-}

Cross-group tie formation is analysed at the agent level using the `cross_group_friends` variable, which records the number of cross-group friends held by each agent at week 52. Since the model does not export full edge lists, the following analysis characterises the *degree distribution* and *assortativity proxies* rather than full graph statistics.

**Degree distribution.** At baseline, the mean cross-group degree per agent is low (migrants: 0.40 friends; locals: 1.16 friends), reflecting the programme's group-size constraint and the low per-session tie-formation rate ($p_{\text{tie}} = 0.05$): each agent can befriend only the cross-group members of its own training group. Of importance, 69--93% of migrants hold zero cross-group friends at week 52 (lowest under RandomBuddy, highest under SuboptimalOpen), a high isolation rate that declines only under scenarios preserving rich group composition. Cross-group degree is lowest under SuboptimalOpen and Suboptimal Composition, and highest under the week-8 buddy scenarios (RandomBuddy, CentralityBuddy), which mandate a cross-group pairing (Figure below).

**Social isolation.** Migrant isolation (zero cross-group friends) reaches 69--93% across scenarios versus 33--87% for locals (Figure below), a structural asymmetry driven by group size (75 migrants vs. 25 locals per programme in the BehaviorSpace experiments). This asymmetry is smallest under Suboptimal Composition, where the scarcity of locals leaves both migrants and locals broadly isolated (the gap collapses to about 4.5 pp), and largest under designs that retain abundant locals, such as Baseline (around 37 pp).

**Assortativity proxies.** Spearman correlations between agent attributes and cross-group degree reveal that initial motivation is the strongest predictor of cross-group tie formation (ρ = 0.43 at baseline), while SES is essentially uncorrelated (ρ = 0.03). Distance to park shows a modest negative association (ρ = −0.14). These results confirm that the social mechanism (motivation → active participation → tie formation) dominates over structural access (SES, distance) in determining integration outcomes.

**Link-prediction structural validation.** As a quantitative test of whether the network module produces ties with learnable triadic structure (rather than randomly placed within training groups), we ran a static link-prediction protocol on the week-52 friendship graphs of four scenarios spanning the design space: Baseline, Suboptimal Composition (sparse network), BuddyProgram (denser, partly mandated cross-group ties), and High SES Heterogeneity. For each run we held out 20% of the edges, built a training graph from the remaining 80%, sampled an equal number of non-edges as negatives, and evaluated four standard predictors using `igraph`: Adamic--Adar [@adamic2003], common neighbours, Jaccard, and preferential attachment. Negative samples were drawn uniformly without replacement from the set of non-edges in the full week-52 graph. Across 964 successfully analysed runs (sparse Suboptimal Composition runs with fewer than 25 edges are excluded by an analysis-stability threshold; only 69 of 300 Suboptimal runs survive this filter, so the highest mean AUC reported below -- Adamic--Adar Suboptimal Composition $= 0.761$ -- reflects the smallest analysed subsample and should be read with that caveat), the three triadic-closure predictors yield mean AUC values between 0.735 and 0.761 in every scenario, all rejecting the chance value 0.5 at $p < 10^{-4}$ in one-sample $t$-tests against the null AUC = 0.5; preferential attachment yields AUC $\approx 0.50$ in every scenario (Baseline 0.499, Suboptimal Composition 0.482, BuddyProgram 0.505, High SES Heterogeneity 0.510; none significantly different from chance after Holm correction across the 16 scenario--predictor cells). The interpretation is substantive: ties in this network form via co-attendance and shared neighbours (triadic closure), not via degree-based "rich get richer" attachment. This is consistent with Granovetter's [-@granovetter1973] weak-tie account and with @centola2007's complex-contagion mechanism for behavioural spread in clustered networks; it is inconsistent with a Barabási--Albert preferential-attachment generative process. We caveat that the model's small training groups and sparse tie formation keep cross-group degree low (Appendix G.2), which compresses degree heterogeneity and would attenuate any preferential-attachment signal even in a graph generated by a BA process; this test is therefore a permissive rather than a discriminating one for ruling out preferential-attachment dynamics in denser networks. Per-scenario AUC summaries are reported in Appendix F.5, the significance tests against chance (AUC = 0.5) in Appendix F.6, and the per-run AUC distribution summary in Appendix F.9. The figure below shows the AUC distribution across scenarios and predictors.

\FloatBarrier

```{r fig_link_prediction, echo=FALSE, fig.scap="Link-prediction AUC by scenario and predictor", fig.cap="Link-prediction AUC across four scenarios and four predictors. Boxplots show the per-run AUC distribution (964 successfully analysed runs out of 1{,}200; 236 runs excluded by the 25-edge threshold, 231 of them sparse Suboptimal Composition). Dashed line: chance value 0.5. Adamic--Adar, common neighbours, and Jaccard all yield AUC $\\approx 0.74$--$0.76$ across all four scenarios with extremely tight per-run distributions; preferential attachment is at chance in every scenario, indicating that degree alone does not predict tie formation in this network.", fig.pos="H", out.width="92%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig_link_prediction.png")
```

\FloatBarrier

```{r fig7_network, fig.scap="Cross-group tie degree distribution", fig.cap="Cross-group tie degree distribution by scenario (all agents × 300–500 runs per scenario). Boxes = median/IQR; whiskers = 1.5×IQR. Suboptimal Composition produces the lowest cross-group degree (mean 0.15 friends); the buddy-pairing variants the highest (RandomBuddy 0.78, CentralityBuddy 0.76), ahead of BuddyProgram (0.70) and High SES Heterogeneity (0.67).", fig.pos="H"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig7_cross_degree_distribution.png")
```

\FloatBarrier

```{r fig8_isolation, fig.scap="Social isolation rate by scenario", fig.cap="Social isolation rate (proportion of agents with zero cross-group friends at week 52) by scenario and agent type. Migrant isolation exceeds local isolation in every scenario; the gap spans 4.5 to 39.8 pp. Under Suboptimal Composition the gap collapses to 4.5 pp because both groups are broadly isolated (locals 86\\%, migrants 91\\%); in the other 22 scenarios the structural group-size asymmetry (75 migrants vs 25 locals) produces gaps of roughly 7 to 40 pp.", fig.pos="H"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig8_isolation_rate.png")
```


## G.3 Structural Network Analysis {-}

The preceding degree-based analysis uses scalar proxies (cross-group friend count, isolation rate) measured from agent-level data. To characterise the *structure* of the emergent friendship networks, we constructed igraph objects from the 5,400 edge-list files exported at week 52 (`CIM_edges_{scenario}_{run}.csv`) and computed six graph-theoretic metrics per run: Louvain modularity $Q$ [@blondel2008], global clustering coefficient, giant component fraction, normalised betweenness centrality, Newman breed assortativity [@newman2003], and mean tie strength by cross-group status. Each scenario is a controlled computational experiment, providing clean identification of the model-world causal effect of scenario design on network structure [@will2020].

**Causal identification.** The ABM provides experimental-quality causal identification at the scenario level: each scenario varies one design parameter with all other inputs held constant and 300 independent random seeds, equivalent to a randomised experiment with no confounders, no attrition, and no non-compliance. Scenario-level effects on both retention and network structure are therefore causally identified within the model world. However, the week-52 network metrics are *post-treatment outcomes* of the retention process itself -- agents who dropped out earlier are absent from the network -- so they cannot serve as independent causal predictors of retention within scenarios. This distinction (causal at the scenario level, associational within scenarios) guides the interpretation of all findings below.

**Network fragmentation.** In most scenarios, only 30--39% of agents belong to the giant component at week 52; the remaining 61--70% are in small disconnected clusters of 2--5 agents or are completely isolated. The exception is RotatingGroups, where group reshuffling every 4 weeks connects 87.8% of agents into a single giant component (Cohen's $d = 5.09$ vs Baseline, $p < 10^{-4}$). This fragmentation explains why the peer influence mechanism (H1) has a relatively modest retention effect ($d = -0.58$): influence can only flow through connected components, and most agents are structurally unreachable.

**Granovetter's weak ties validated.** Cross-group ties are significantly weaker than within-group ties in 14 of the 16 original scenarios after Holm correction (pooled $d = -0.41$, $p < 10^{-4}$); the Appendix G.3 structural network analysis was conducted on the 5,400 edge files exported from the original 16-scenario campaign and not re-run for the Phase 3 robustness extensions. The bridging ties that connect migrant and local communities are weak ties, consistent with Granovetter's [-@granovetter1973] foundational prediction. Within cross-group ties, weaker ties have *higher* edge betweenness ($\rho = -0.43$, $p < 10^{-4}$, $N = 1{,}179$ edges), confirming that the weakest cross-group ties serve as the most important structural bridges [@everett2016]. One scenario reverses this pattern: under Suboptimal Composition, the structural scarcity of locals (1 per park) concentrates interaction on the few available cross-group partners, producing stronger-than-within cross-ties ($d = 0.20$, $p < 0.001$ after Holm correction).

\FloatBarrier

```{r fig_sna_weak_ties, echo=FALSE, fig.scap="Mean tie strength by tie type and scenario", fig.cap="Mean tie strength at week 52 by tie type (cross-group vs within-group) and scenario. Cross-group ties are weaker in 14 of the 16 original scenarios (Holm-corrected; Phase 3 scenarios not included in this SNA analysis); the exception is Suboptimal Composition, where structural scarcity of locals concentrates interaction on cross-group partners. Consistent with Granovetter (1973): bridging ties are weak ties.", fig.pos="H", out.width="90%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig_sna_weak_ties.png")
```

**BuddyProgram: motivational, not structural.** Equivalence testing (TOST, $\delta = 0.05$) confirms that BuddyProgram's network structure is indistinguishable from Baseline on all four metrics: modularity ($\Delta = -0.001$, TOST $p < 0.001$), clustering ($\Delta = +0.003$), giant component ($\Delta = -0.001$), and assortativity ($\Delta = -0.018$, TOST $p = 0.001$). Yet BuddyProgram achieves +3.4 pp higher retention ($t = 9.03$, $p < 10^{-4}$). Because the network mediator is provably unchanged while the outcome changes, the retention effect must operate through the direct motivational pathway (the 8-week buddy anchor) rather than through network restructuring. This is mediation by exclusion: path $a \approx 0$ in the Baron--Kenny framework, so the indirect effect is zero regardless of path $b$.

**RotatingGroups: structural integration at a motivational cost.** RotatingGroups produces massive network restructuring: modularity $Q$ drops from 0.708 (Baseline) to 0.575 ($d = -2.13$), clustering from 0.44 to 0.20 ($d = -2.38$), and giant component rises from 30.7% to 87.8% ($d = +5.09$). All effects are significant at $p < 10^{-4}$ with 95% CIs excluding zero. Yet retention is 2.3 pp *lower* than Baseline (41.7% vs 45.1%). Two causal pathways operate in opposition: the structural pathway (more connectivity, better peer influence reach) acts toward higher retention, but the motivational pathway (destruction of stable training groups every 4 weeks) acts toward lower retention. The motivational disruption dominates, producing the net negative effect.

\FloatBarrier

```{r fig_sna_modularity, echo=FALSE, fig.scap="Network modularity by scenario", fig.cap="Network modularity (Louvain) by scenario. Lower Q indicates more integrated network structure. RotatingGroups achieves the lowest modularity (Q=0.57) through group reshuffling but at the cost of reduced retention. BuddyProgram is structurally indistinguishable from Baseline (TOST equivalence p<0.001).", fig.pos="H", out.width="90%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig_sna_modularity.png")
```

**Contact Hypothesis mechanism confirmed.** Within the Baseline scenario ($N = 22{,}500$ migrant agents), weeks attended correlates strongly with cross-group friends ($\rho = +0.66$, $p \approx 0$), and distance to park correlates negatively ($\rho = -0.15$, $p < 10^{-4}$). Structured co-attendance at training sessions is the primary driver of cross-group tie formation, consistent with Allport's [-@allport1954] conditions and the Contact Hypothesis meta-analysis [@pettigrew2006].

```{r sna_table, echo=FALSE}
sna_tbl <- read.csv("tables/table_sna_metrics.csv")
sna_display <- sna_tbl %>%
  select(Scenario = scenario, N = n_runs,
         `Mod. Q` = mod_q_mean,
         `Clust.` = cc_mean,
         `GC frac.` = gc_frac_mean,
         `Assort.` = assort_mean) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric) & !matches("N"), ~round(.x, 3)))
knitr::kable(sna_display, booktabs = TRUE, format = "latex",
  caption = "Structural network metrics by scenario (mean across runs).", caption.short = "Structural network metrics by scenario",
  align = c("l","r","r","r","r","r")) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 8, latex_options = "hold_position") |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = "Mod. Q = Louvain modularity; Clust. = global clustering coefficient; GC frac. = giant-component fraction; Assort. = Newman breed assortativity (negative = disassortative = more cross-group mixing).",
    general_title = "Column key: ",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE, threeparttable = TRUE, escape = TRUE
  )
```

**Caveats.** These networks are small (approximately 100 nodes, 30--60 edges per run) and sparse, so absolute values of modularity and betweenness should be interpreted comparatively across scenarios rather than against benchmarks from large-scale empirical networks. The week-52 snapshot captures surviving ties only; agents who dropped out earlier are absent from the network, creating survivorship bias in all structural metrics. Within-scenario correlations between network metrics and retention are near-zero (all $\rho < 0.09$), confirming that the feedback loop between retention and network structure prevents within-scenario causal identification. The scenario-level comparisons (where one design parameter is experimentally varied) remain the primary source of causal claims.


## G.4 Framework-generality and Robustness Findings {-}

This section reports the framework-generality and robustness extensions introduced in the v6.4 release: sensitivity-analysis provenance, gamma-bracket robustness, open-population dynamics, and a second-domain portability test against a Berlin language-course calibration. Each extension is motivated by a question raised during Tier 1 peer review or by the programme director's suggestion that the model-world tool should be evaluated as a generalisable framework rather than an Istanbul-specific case study.

### Sensitivity-analysis provenance and version-invariance {-}

The dominant sensitivity result, $\text{PRCC}(\gamma, \text{retention}) = -0.96$, is computed under `CIM_v6_4.nlogo` (Section 4.4). This section documents that the estimate is version-invariant *by construction*: the sweep exercises a code path that is byte-identical across the v6.2--v6.4 releases, so the same value would be obtained under any of them.

The `Sensitivity_3level` BehaviorSpace experiment runs the Baseline scenario at $3^4 = 81$ combinations of $(\beta, \gamma, p_{\text{tie}}, \theta_d)$ with 10 replicates each, for 810 runs total. The executed code path in the `go` procedure is byte-identical across v6.2, v6.3, and v6.4. Between v6.2 and v6.3 the calibrated parameter values and baseline scenario configuration were unchanged. Between v6.3 and v6.4 the Tier 2 refactor introduced a configuration-file loader and 28 `cfg-*` globals (26 in the original Tier 2 commit; 2 added for the Phase 3 OpenPopulation extension, `cfg-inflow-rate-per-week` and `cfg-outflow-rate-per-week`) whose defaults match the v6.3 literals; the Baseline run at seed 42 is bit-identical between v6.3 and v6.4 (T2.4 regression test).

Because every code path exercised by the sensitivity experiment is byte-identical across versions, the joint output distribution is version-invariant: the $\text{PRCC}(\gamma, \text{retention}) = -0.96$ value computed from the v6.4 sweep is the same value the v6.2 and v6.3 code paths would produce. This is a code-path-identity argument anchored on the v6.3$\leftrightarrow$v6.4 bit-identical regression test, not a claim of three independent re-runs.

### Gamma-bracket ranking robustness {-}

The motivation-decay-rate $\gamma$ is calibrated at 0.018 per week from @gjestvang2020. Classmate peer review raised the concern that this value, drawn from a Norwegian supervised-fitness-club cohort, may not transfer to migrants under compounding stressors. The gamma-bracket sweep evaluates $\gamma \in \{0.011, 0.018, 0.025\}$ -- the central value plus a $\pm 40\%$ bracket -- across three scenarios (Baseline, BuddyProgram, Suboptimal Composition), with 300 runs per cell:

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\caption[Gamma-bracket robustness of retention]{Retention (\%) under a $\pm 40\%$ bracket on the motivation-decay rate $\gamma$ (300 runs per cell, three scenarios).}
\footnotesize
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
 & BuddyProgram & Baseline & Suboptimal Comp. \\
\midrule
$\gamma = 0.011$ (low)     & 75.4\% & 71.9\% & 56.2\% \\
$\gamma = 0.018$ (central) & 48.5\% & 45.1\% & 27.9\% \\
$\gamma = 0.025$ (high)    & 23.4\% & 21.1\% & 11.1\% \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The ranking `BuddyProgram > Baseline > Suboptimal Composition` holds at all three values of $\gamma$. The decay rate scales retention multiplicatively, consistent with its role as a uniform first-order loss term, and does not reverse scenario orderings within the Istanbul-calisthenics calibration. The bracket is robust in rank but sensitive in level: a mis-calibration of $\gamma$ by $\pm 40\%$ shifts Baseline retention from 21.1\% to 71.9\%, underscoring that model-derived absolute estimates should be read as model-world outputs, not real-world forecasts.

### Open-population dynamics {-}

The v6.4 model assumes a closed population: no arrivals, no departures during the 52-week programme. To test whether this simplification drives retention estimates, the `OpenPopulation` scenario adds weekly Bernoulli probabilities of one new arrival ($p_{\text{in}} = 0.30$) and one random active-participant departure ($p_{\text{out}} = 0.20$). Arrival and departure at these rates correspond to roughly 15 arrivals and 10 departures over the year on a base of 75 migrants. The full six-outcome comparison against closed-population Baseline (300 runs each; Welch $t$-tests, Holm-corrected within this six-outcome comparison) reveals a differential pattern.

**Quantitative outcomes shift; one network-structure metric is invariant.** Retention falls $-4.5$ percentage points under open population (40.6% vs 45.1%; Cohen's $d = -0.82$, large; Holm-adjusted $p < 10^{-4}$), and language CEFR gain falls by 0.195 levels ($d = -1.44$, large; $p < 10^{-4}$) -- the largest single shift produced by any robustness perturbation, larger than any of the H1--H4 confirmatory manipulations on CEFR. Mean motivation falls $-0.016$ ($d = -0.34$, small; Holm $p < 10^{-4}$). Female and male dropout rates rise symmetrically by $+4.4$ and $+4.4$ percentage points ($d = +0.64$, $+0.59$; Holm $p < 10^{-4}$ for both). In contrast, the **cross-group tie ratio is essentially unchanged** (0.499 vs 0.502; $d = -0.03$; $p = 0.69$, not significant). To verify whether this single-metric finding generalises, a follow-up multi-metric SNA comparison (\path{R/28_open_pop_sna.R}) computed five additional metric families (six graph-theoretic tests) on the 600 OpenPopulation and Baseline edge files: Louvain modularity $Q$ (Baseline 0.706 vs OpenPopulation 0.702; $d = -0.065$), global clustering coefficient (0.438 vs 0.425; $d = -0.125$), giant-component fraction (0.307 vs 0.311; $d = +0.058$), Newman breed assortativity ($-0.044$ vs $-0.053$; $d = -0.060$), and mean tie strength split by cross-group status (cross-group: 0.348 vs 0.341, $d = -0.16$; within-group: 0.360 vs 0.356, $d = -0.09$). **None of the six graph-structure metrics differs significantly** between the two scenarios after Holm correction within the 6-test family (all six effects negligible, $|d| \le 0.16$; all Holm-adjusted $p > 0.2$; full table in Appendix F.7). The network metrics tested -- modularity, clustering, fragmentation, breed-mixing, and tie-strength composition -- are invariant to the tested open-cohort regime; we caveat that other graph-structure properties not tested (degree-distribution shape, network diameter, k-core decomposition, motif counts, temporal trajectory of these metrics across weeks rather than at week-52 only) cannot be ruled in or out from this analysis. By contrast, the time-dependent quantitative outcomes (retention, language gain) shift in the direction predicted by the mechanism (new arrivals enter mid-programme with shorter exposure windows, mechanically reducing average language proficiency and pushing the mean retention down). Note that the inflow rate $p_{\text{in}} = 0.30$ exceeds $p_{\text{out}} = 0.20$ (a net-inflow asymmetry); we have not tested the symmetric or net-outflow regime.

The closed-population simplification therefore does not drive the main qualitative Baseline finding. To directly test whether the *Baseline-vs-Suboptimal* ranking preserves under churn, we ran the additional `SuboptimalOpen` scenario (1 local per park combined with $p_{\text{in}} = 0.30$ / $p_{\text{out}} = 0.20$ inflow/outflow rates). Across 300 runs, SuboptimalOpen retention reaches 26.00% versus 27.89% for Suboptimal Composition (closed); the open-population effect on the Suboptimal scenario is a smaller absolute drop than on Baseline ($-1.89$ pp vs $-4.46$ pp; Cohen's $d = -0.41$, small; Holm-adjusted $p < 10^{-4}$ within a 36-test 4-scenario family; the full pairwise comparison is reported in Appendix F.10). Critically, the **Baseline-vs-Suboptimal ranking is preserved in BOTH cohort regimes** (an H2-specific test; H1, H3, H4 manipulations were not re-run under open population): Baseline (45.07%) > Suboptimal (27.89%) under closed cohort with a 17.18 pp gap; OpenPopulation (40.61%) > SuboptimalOpen (26.00%) under open cohort with a 14.60 pp gap. The gap *attenuates by 2.57 pp* (a 15.0\% relative reduction) under the calibrated churn rates -- a directional signal that the composition effect is partially dampened by churn; we do not bracket the churn rate at which the ranking would break, and we explicitly limit this finding to H2 rather than implying it generalises to H1, H3, H4. The remaining within-regime contrasts are highly significant ($d = -3.31$ closed Baseline vs Suboptimal and $d = -2.95$ open OpenPopulation vs SuboptimalOpen, both Holm-$p < 10^{-4}$). The overall pattern -- six-of-six graph-structure metrics invariant to churn, AND the headline composition-ranking preserved across cohort regimes -- provides a defensible robustness narrative: structural integration claims (modularity, clustering, bridging-tie composition) and headline policy ranks survive realistic open-cohort dynamics, while absolute language-gain forecasts are mechanically sensitive to cohort turnover and should be interpreted as model-world outputs rather than real-world point estimates. Programmes with higher turnover than tested, or with net-outflow rather than net-inflow, would require independent calibration.

\FloatBarrier

```{r fig_open_pop, echo=FALSE, fig.scap="Open-population robustness (six outcomes)", fig.cap="Open-population robustness. Six-outcome comparison of closed-cohort Baseline ($n = 300$) versus open-cohort OpenPopulation ($n = 300$, weekly $p_{\\text{in}} = 0.30$, $p_{\\text{out}} = 0.20$). Bars: mean; error bars: 95\\% CI. Retention, motivation, language CEFR gain, and dropout rates shift by significant amounts under churn ($|d|$ from 0.34 to 1.44); cross-group tie ratio is essentially unchanged ($d = -0.03$, $p = 0.69$). The differential pattern -- quantitative outcomes shift, qualitative network structure preserves -- is documented in the per-outcome and comparative-magnitude tables saved to the tables directory.", fig.pos="H", out.width="92%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig_open_pop.png")
```

### Berlin language-course: a second-domain test {-}

To test whether the framework-level claims transfer beyond community sport, the v6.4 release implements a second-domain calibration representing Berlin Federal Office for Migration and Refugees (BAMF) integration courses [@kosyakova2021]. The Berlin configuration differs from Istanbul on 35 of 44 parameter rows (the nine shared values include the peer-influence coefficient $\beta = 0.08$ and the initial tie strength 0.20): a lower motivation-decay rate (0.015 per week) offset by funded-attendance enforcement, a higher CEFR gain rate (0.024 vs 0.019 per hour) reflecting formal instruction, a one-instructor-per-twenty-migrant classroom in place of peer-dense community groups, absence of an outdoor-season regime (indoor year-round), and denser urban transit reducing distance barriers. Thirteen scenarios were run in the Berlin configuration with 300 replicates each, for 3,900 runs total. The three non-transferable scenarios (No Indoor Continuity, NoIndoor Minimal, Winter50) are marked N/A.

Five pre-registered ranking relations from the Istanbul analysis were tested in both domains; four (Suboptimal Composition < Baseline, Suboptimal Composition < BuddyProgram, Minimal Support < Baseline, Baseline < BuddyProgram) were fully testable, while the fifth (NoIndoor Minimal < Baseline) is Istanbul-only because NoIndoor Minimal is N/A in Berlin. All four are overwhelmingly significant in the home domain (Istanbul Welch $t$-tests, every $p < 10^{-4}$), so Berlin is a fair transfer test. In Berlin, **only two of the four transfer**. Minimal Support < Baseline holds decisively: support-service removal lowers retention from 67.0\% to 51.2\% ($-15.9$ pp, Cohen's $d = 3.40$, $p < 10^{-4}$). Suboptimal Composition < BuddyProgram holds marginally ($+0.8$ pp, $d = 0.18$, one-sided $p = 0.03$). The remaining two are directionally consistent but statistically flat: **Suboptimal Composition < Baseline** -- which in Istanbul-calisthenics is a large effect, the 5-to-1 local-per-park reduction lowering retention from 45.1\% to 27.9\% ($-17.2$ pp, $d = -3.31$) -- shrinks in Berlin to 66.7\% against a Baseline of 67.0\% (a $-0.3$ pp difference, $p = 0.42$, not significant); and **Baseline < BuddyProgram** likewise shrinks to 67.0\% vs 67.5\% ($+0.5$ pp, $p = 0.18$, not significant). In the funding-enforced classroom the higher-performing designs cluster tightly at 66--68\% retention and become statistically indistinguishable: the fine-grained ordering among good designs does not transfer, and only the gross distinction between providing and withholding support survives.

```{r hero_fig, out.width="95%", fig.scap="Ranking matrix: Istanbul vs. Berlin", fig.cap="Ranking matrix across Istanbul-calisthenics (top row) and Berlin-language-course (bottom row). Cells report mean retention rate (\\%); fill colour encodes retention on a shared scale across both rows. Of four fully-testable ranking relations, only two transfer to Berlin (Minimal Support < Baseline, $d = 3.40$; and, marginally, Suboptimal Composition < BuddyProgram). The two relations among the top-clustered designs -- Suboptimal Composition vs Baseline (66.7 vs 67.0) and Baseline vs BuddyProgram (67.0 vs 67.5) -- collapse to non-significant 0.3--0.5 pp differences in Berlin. Three scenarios (No Indoor Continuity, NoIndoor Minimal, Winter50) are Istanbul-only and marked N/A in the Berlin row.", echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics("outputs/hero_figure_2x16_ranking.pdf")
```

The interpretation is a partial-generality finding. Across a community-sport context and a state-funded classroom context that differ on most calibrated parameters, the *gross* ranking transfers -- providing versus withholding support (Minimal Support < Baseline) is a large, significant effect in both domains -- but the *fine-grained* ordering among the better designs does not. The two collapsing relations identify a structural boundary common to all peer-mediated levers: both the Suboptimal Composition manipulation and the BuddyProgram premium operate in Istanbul through the local peer pool from which peer-influence and tie-formation opportunities arise; in the Berlin classroom, attendance is funding-conditional and teacher-led rather than peer-reinforced, so altering the number or pairing of non-migrant peers has a muted effect on the dropout pathway. The mechanism is domain-consistent -- it simply finds no purchase in a domain where its input signal is structurally suppressed. This is informative for programme designers: the cost of violating balanced group composition, and the benefit of buddy pairing, are both highest in peer-influence-dominated venues and negligible where attendance is externally enforced.


## G.5 Mechanism Robustness Check: Alternative Mechanism Benchmark {-}

To assess whether the model's outcomes are specific to the tie-weighted peer influence mechanism or could emerge from alternative motivation dynamics, we tested a **contact-presence contagion** mechanism. In this alternative specification, motivation spreads as a function of the fraction of group members attending each week (independent of tie strength) rather than through the weighted social network. This benchmark tests whether the assumed motivation mechanism is consequential or whether a simpler alternative produces equivalent macro-level outcomes.

```{r equifinality_load, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
eq_tbl <- readRDS("data/equifinality_comparison.rds")
```

```{r equifinality_table, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(knitr)
eq_display <- eq_tbl %>%
  select(metric, baseline_mean, equifin_mean, diff, cohens_d, p_value) %>%
  mutate(
    p_fmt = ifelse(p_value < 0.001, "< 0.001", sprintf("%.3f", p_value)),
    sig   = case_when(p_value < 0.001 ~ "***",
                      p_value < 0.01  ~ "**",
                      p_value < 0.05  ~ "*",
                      TRUE            ~ "")
  ) %>%
  select(Metric = metric,
         `Baseline (mean)` = baseline_mean,
         `Contact-contagion (mean)` = equifin_mean,
         `Difference` = diff,
         `Cohen's d` = cohens_d,
         `p_value` = p_fmt,
         ` ` = sig)

knitr::kable(eq_display, booktabs = TRUE, format = "latex",
      caption = "Mechanism comparison: tie-weighted peer influence (Baseline) vs contact-presence contagion (Alternative Mechanism Benchmark).", caption.short = "Mechanism comparison (peer influence vs. contagion)",
      align = c("l","r","r","r","r","r","l")) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 8, latex_options = "hold_position") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(1, width = "3.2cm") |>
  kableExtra::footnote(
    general = "Welch t-test. Retention, motivation, cost, and both dropout rates differ at p < 0.001; language CEFR gain (p = 0.32) and cross-group tie ratio (p = 0.71) do not differ significantly. Significance: *** p < 0.001, ** p < 0.01, * p < 0.05.",
    general_title = "Note: ",
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE, threeparttable = TRUE, escape = TRUE
  )
```

The two mechanisms produce **substantially different outcomes** (Table above). The contact-presence contagion model generates 12.5 percentage points higher retention (57.5% vs 45.1%, Cohen's *d* = 2.24, *p* < 0.001), 23% higher average motivation, and 22% lower cost per retained participant. Notably, the divergence is confined to the motivation--retention--cost pathway: language CEFR gain (*p* = 0.32) and cross-group tie ratio (*p* = 0.71) are statistically indistinguishable between the two mechanisms, so the contact-presence specification inflates motivation and retention without altering the network structure or the language trajectory. The motivation trajectories diverge from the first weeks of the simulation (Figure below), indicating a structural difference in dynamics rather than a convergent end-state.

```{r equifinality_fig, echo=FALSE, fig.scap="Motivation trajectories: peer influence vs. contagion", fig.cap="Motivation trajectories under tie-weighted peer influence (Baseline, blue) vs contact-presence contagion (Alternative Mechanism Benchmark, red). Shaded bands show interquartile range across runs. The mechanisms diverge from the outset and do not converge.", out.width="90%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/15_equifinality_trajectories.png")
```

The two specifications are therefore **not equifinal**: the choice of motivation model is consequential. The tie-weighted peer influence mechanism produces more conservative, empirically grounded estimates, consistent with the social capital literature showing that tie *quality* (not mere co-presence) drives sustained behavioural change [@putnam2000]. The contact-presence specification, while simpler, implicitly assumes that passive attendance is as motivating as active social bonding, which is not supported by the sport-integration literature. We therefore retain the tie-weighted mechanism as the primary model specification. We note, however, that the comparison conflates mechanism *type* (network-mediated vs. mass-contact) with mechanism *strength* (the contact-presence specification has a larger effective influence pool). A fully controlled comparison would equalise total influence magnitude before testing structural differences; this remains an avenue for future work.




## G.6 Inference-robustness checks (difference-in-differences and randomization inference) {-}

**Difference-in-differences validation.** Two time-varying-treatment scenarios admit a parallel-trends test: No Indoor Continuity and Winter50 differ from Baseline only at indoor-season onset (week 9). The pre-treatment period (weeks 1--8) is identical by construction; pre-treatment mean motivation is 0.558 across all three scenarios with placebo within-pre DiD = $+0.0005$ (parallel-trends assumption holds by construction, since the pre-period is identical across scenarios, not by empirical test). At indoor-season onset, trajectories diverge: treatment-period DiD = $-0.049$ for No Indoor Continuity and $-0.013$ for Winter50 (consistent with the latter's milder manipulation: 50% indoor-partner availability versus full removal). Post-treatment DiD (weeks 29--52) is substantially larger than the treatment-period DiD ($-0.147$ for No Indoor Continuity; $-0.034$ for Winter50), reflecting cumulative dropout cascades that persist after the treatment formally ends. This DiD is a mechanism-timing validation that corroborates, rather than independently identifies, the scenario-level effects: the trends break exactly when, and only when, the treatment fires, while the pre-treatment placebo is near-zero by construction.

```{r fig_did_parallel_trends, echo=FALSE, fig.scap="Difference-in-differences parallel-trends test", fig.cap="Difference-in-differences parallel-trends test for time-varying scenarios. Mean motivation among active agents per week, averaged across N = 300 runs per scenario; ribbons show $\\pm 1$ SE across runs (very narrow at this $N$). Treatment period (weeks 9--28, indoor season) is shaded. Pre-treatment trends (weeks 1--8) are identical by construction, supporting the parallel-trends assumption; the post-treatment DiD persists and compounds after winter onset (largest for No Indoor Continuity). Full DiD estimates are reported in Appendix G.6.", fig.pos="H", out.width="92%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig_did_parallel_trends.png")
```

**Randomization-inference robustness check.** As a second inference layer that does not rely on the normal approximation underlying Welch's $t$-test, we ran randomization-inference (RI) permutation tests on all 24 confirmatory comparisons (4 scenarios $\times$ 6 outcomes; 10,000 permutations each; sharp-null hypothesis $\tau_i = 0$ for all units; two-sided $p$-values; Holm correction applied within the confirmatory family, matching the methodology used for the parametric tests above). RI agreed with Welch+Holm on significance for **24 of 24 tests** (100% agreement). The 19 tests significant under Welch+Holm are all significant under RI+Holm; the 5 borderline non-significant tests (Weak Peer Influence on motivation, language, and cross-tie ratio; Minimal Support on motivation and language) are non-significant under RI as well. RI's $p$-value resolution is bounded below by $1/(N_{\text{perm}} + 1) \approx 10^{-4}$, so for the most extreme tests (e.g., Suboptimal Composition retention with Welch $p < 10^{-4}$) RI cannot match the parametric resolution but confirms the qualitative rejection. The full RI vs Welch+Holm comparison is reported in Appendix F.1; the underlying permutation distributions (10,000 samples per test) are non-tabular and are retained with the analysis outputs rather than reproduced inline.
